title: Therapeutic Patient Positioning
subtitle: The nurse matches position to purpose — high-Fowler's for breathing, eating, and tube feeding; side-lying to protect the airway and offload the sacrum — while keeping alignment and repositioning every 2 hours.

patient.name: Patient Positioning
patient.admitDate: The nurse positions patients therapeutically for breathing, safety, comfort, and skin protection.

nursesNote: The nurse positions patients to match the clinical purpose. High-Fowler's (60-90 degrees) eases breathing and is used for eating and during and after tube feedings to prevent aspiration, while semi-Fowler's (30-45 degrees) provides comfort and reduces reflux. A side-lying (lateral) position offloads the sacrum and protects the airway of an unconscious or drowsy patient by letting secretions drain, and the Sims position is used for enemas and recovery. Prone positioning can improve oxygenation in severe respiratory failure, and the orthopneic position (sitting and leaning over a table) relieves severe dyspnea. The nurse keeps the body in neutral alignment, supports joints and pressure points with pillows, repositions immobile patients about every 2 hours, and lifts rather than drags the patient to prevent shear and skin injury.
providerOrders: Place patients in high-Fowler's for dyspnea, meals, and tube feedings
Use a side-lying position to protect the airway of a drowsy patient
Keep the head of bed elevated during and after tube feedings
Reposition immobile patients about every 2 hours
Maintain body alignment and lift rather than drag the patient

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about patient positioning?

opts.text: High-Fowler's position helps a patient who is short of breath
A side-lying position helps protect the airway of an unconscious patient
The head of the bed is elevated during and after tube feeding
A patient should lie flat right after eating to aid digestion
Immobile patients are repositioned about every 2 hours
Pillows are used to support alignment and pressure points
Lying flat increases the risk of aspiration during a tube feeding
The orthopneic position (sitting, leaning forward) eases severe dyspnea
Prone positioning can improve oxygenation in severe respiratory failure
Dragging a patient across the sheets reduces skin injury
The Sims (side-lying) position is used for enemas
Maintaining body alignment prevents contractures and discomfort

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order repositioning a patient to side-lying

opts.text: Explain the plan and raise the bed to working height
Get help and lower the side rail as appropriate
Roll the patient toward you onto the side
Support the back, head, and legs with pillows
Check alignment, skin, and comfort
A patient in acute respiratory distress
An unconscious patient to protect the airway
During and after a tube feeding
Relieving pressure on the sacrum
A patient eating a meal in bed
A drowsy patient recovering from anesthesia
Maximizing lung expansion for shortness of breath
Draining oral secretions in a sedated patient

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient becomes short of breath lying flat
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Raise the head of the bed to high-Fowler's
Assess respirations and oxygen saturation
Apply oxygen if indicated or ordered
Stay with the patient and reassess
Notify the provider if there is no improvement
Keeping the patient in correct body alignment
Leaving a patient flat during a tube feeding
Repositioning an immobile patient about every 2 hours
Placing an unconscious patient flat without airway protection
Supporting joints and pressure points with pillows
Dragging the patient up in bed across the sheets
Elevating the head of bed for a patient with dyspnea
Positioning a drowsy post-op patient side-lying
